name: George Washington
notes:
#1 · 1789–1797

Commander of the Continental Army in the Revolutionary War and the first president of the United States.
He set enduring precedents, including a formal cabinet and the two-term tradition, and steered the young republic to neutrality abroad.

name: John Adams
notes:
#2 · 1797–1801

A Founding Father and the first vice president, Adams was a leading Federalist.
He kept the nation out of full war with France during the Quasi-War but drew fire for the Alien and Sedition Acts.

name: Thomas Jefferson
notes:
#3 · 1801–1809

Principal author of the Declaration of Independence.
As president he doubled the country with the Louisiana Purchase and dispatched the Lewis and Clark Expedition.

name: James Madison
notes:
#4 · 1809–1817

Known as the Father of the Constitution and a co-author of the Federalist Papers.
He led the United States through the War of 1812 against Britain.

name: James Monroe
notes:
#5 · 1817–1825

His presidency is called the Era of Good Feelings.
He issued the Monroe Doctrine, warning European powers against further colonization of the Americas.

name: John Quincy Adams
notes:
#6 · 1825–1829

Son of John Adams and a distinguished diplomat who negotiated the Treaty of Ghent.
He championed internal improvements, then served nearly two decades in Congress as a fierce opponent of slavery.

name: Andrew Jackson
notes:
#7 · 1829–1837

A populist war hero nicknamed Old Hickory who reshaped the presidency and the Democratic Party.
He dismantled the national bank and signed the Indian Removal Act, leading to the Trail of Tears.

name: Martin Van Buren
notes:
#8 · 1837–1841

A skilled political organizer who helped build the modern Democratic Party.
His single term was consumed by the economic depression of the Panic of 1837.

name: William Henry Harrison
notes:
#9 · 1841

A general famed for the Battle of Tippecanoe.
He died of illness just 31 days into office, the shortest presidency in U.S. history.

name: John Tyler
notes:
#10 · 1841–1845

The first vice president to succeed on a president's death, establishing the succession precedent.
He annexed Texas near the end of his term and was largely abandoned by his party.

name: James K. Polk
notes:
#11 · 1845–1849

A driven expansionist who fulfilled Manifest Destiny.
The Mexican–American War added California and the Southwest, and he settled the Oregon boundary with Britain.

name: Zachary Taylor
notes:
#12 · 1849–1850

A Mexican–American War hero called Old Rough and Ready.
He died after 16 months in office as the slavery crisis intensified.

name: Millard Fillmore
notes:
#13 · 1850–1853

Succeeded Taylor and signed the Compromise of 1850.
Its Fugitive Slave Act enraged the North and hastened sectional conflict.

name: Franklin Pierce
notes:
#14 · 1853–1857

A Northern Democrat sympathetic to Southern interests.
He signed the Kansas–Nebraska Act, which reopened the question of slavery in the territories.

name: James Buchanan
notes:
#15 · 1857–1861

The last president before the Civil War.
He viewed secession as illegal but believed he lacked power to stop it, and is often ranked among the worst presidents.

name: Abraham Lincoln
notes:
#16 · 1861–1865

Led the Union through the Civil War and preserved the nation.
He issued the Emancipation Proclamation and championed the Thirteenth Amendment, and was assassinated days after the war's end.

name: Andrew Johnson
notes:
#17 · 1865–1869

A Southern Unionist who succeeded Lincoln.
His lenient Reconstruction clashed with Radical Republicans, and he became the first president to be impeached — acquitted by one vote.

name: Ulysses S. Grant
notes:
#18 · 1869–1877

The Union's commanding general who won the Civil War.
As president he enforced Reconstruction and pursued civil rights, though his administration was dogged by scandal.

name: Rutherford B. Hayes
notes:
#19 · 1877–1881

Won the bitterly disputed 1876 election.
The Compromise of 1877 that secured his victory ended Reconstruction in the South.

name: James A. Garfield
notes:
#20 · 1881

A Civil War general and scholar.
He was shot months into his term and died weeks later, spurring civil-service reform.

name: Chester A. Arthur
notes:
#21 · 1881–1885

A machine politician who unexpectedly became a reformer after succeeding Garfield.
He signed the Pendleton Civil Service Reform Act, ending much of the spoils system.

name: Grover Cleveland
notes:
#22 & 24 · 1885–1889, 1893–1897

The only president to serve two non-consecutive terms.
A fiscal conservative and reformer, his second term was overwhelmed by the Panic of 1893.

name: Benjamin Harrison
notes:
#23 · 1889–1893

Grandson of William Henry Harrison.
He signed the Sherman Antitrust Act and oversaw the admission of six new states.

name: William McKinley
notes:
#25 · 1897–1901

Led the nation to victory in the Spanish–American War, making the U.S. a world power.
He was assassinated early in his second term.

name: Theodore Roosevelt
notes:
#26 · 1901–1909

At 42 the youngest to assume the office, a trust-busting progressive and conservationist.
He brokered the Panama Canal and won the Nobel Peace Prize for ending the Russo-Japanese War.

name: William Howard Taft
notes:
#27 · 1909–1913

Continued antitrust enforcement but split the Republican Party.
He is the only person to serve as both president and Chief Justice of the United States.

name: Woodrow Wilson
notes:
#28 · 1913–1921

A progressive who created the Federal Reserve.
He led the U.S. through World War I and championed the League of Nations, though the Senate rejected it.

name: Warren G. Harding
notes:
#29 · 1921–1923

Won on a promise of a return to normalcy after the war.
He died in office, and his administration was later stained by the Teapot Dome scandal.

name: Calvin Coolidge
notes:
#30 · 1923–1929

Quiet and frugal, nicknamed Silent Cal.
He presided over the prosperity of the Roaring Twenties and favored limited government.

name: Herbert Hoover
notes:
#31 · 1929–1933

A celebrated humanitarian engineer before the presidency.
The Great Depression began months into his term, and his response was widely judged inadequate.

name: Franklin D. Roosevelt
notes:
#32 · 1933–1945

The only president elected four times.
His New Deal reshaped the government during the Depression, and he led the Allies through most of World War II.

name: Harry S. Truman
notes:
#33 · 1945–1953

Ended World War II by authorizing the atomic bombings of Japan.
He launched the Marshall Plan and NATO, desegregated the armed forces, and led the country into the Korean War.

name: Dwight D. Eisenhower
notes:
#34 · 1953–1961

Supreme Allied Commander in Europe in World War II.
As president he ended the Korean War, built the Interstate Highway System, and warned of the military-industrial complex.

name: John F. Kennedy
notes:
#35 · 1961–1963

Faced down the Soviet Union in the Cuban Missile Crisis.
He set the goal of landing on the Moon and was assassinated in Dallas in November 1963.

name: Lyndon B. Johnson
notes:
#36 · 1963–1969

Passed the landmark Civil Rights and Voting Rights Acts and the Great Society programs.
His presidency was ultimately consumed by the escalation of the Vietnam War.

name: Richard Nixon
notes:
#37 · 1969–1974

Opened relations with China and pursued détente with the Soviet Union.
He ended U.S. involvement in Vietnam but resigned in disgrace over the Watergate scandal.

name: Gerald Ford
notes:
#38 · 1974–1977

The only person to serve as president without being elected president or vice president.
He pardoned Nixon and grappled with stagflation and the end of the Vietnam War.

name: Jimmy Carter
notes:
#39 · 1977–1981

Brokered the Camp David Accords between Egypt and Israel.
His term was strained by an energy crisis and the Iran hostage crisis; he later became a celebrated humanitarian.

name: Ronald Reagan
notes:
#40 · 1981–1989

A former actor whose conservative agenda cut taxes and expanded the military.
His later arms agreements with Gorbachev helped wind down the Cold War.

name: George H. W. Bush
notes:
#41 · 1989–1993

A WWII pilot, diplomat, and CIA director before the presidency.
He oversaw the peaceful end of the Cold War and led the coalition that won the Gulf War.

name: Bill Clinton
notes:
#42 · 1993–2001

Presided over a long economic boom and budget surpluses.
He signed welfare reform and NAFTA, and was impeached and acquitted.

name: George W. Bush
notes:
#43 · 2001–2009

Responded to the September 11 attacks by launching the war on terror.
He led wars in Afghanistan and Iraq, and his second term ended amid the 2008 financial crisis.

name: Barack Obama
notes:
#44 · 2009–2017

The first Black president of the United States.
He signed the Affordable Care Act and guided the recovery from the Great Recession.

name: Donald Trump
notes:
#45 & 47 · 2017–2021, 2025–

A businessman and the first president to serve two non-consecutive terms since Cleveland.
His first term featured tax cuts and two impeachments, both ending in acquittal.

name: Joe Biden
notes:
#46 · 2021–2025

A longtime senator and vice president under Obama.
He signed major infrastructure and climate legislation and oversaw the withdrawal from Afghanistan.
